fragments.content: Headache disorders are among the most common neurological complaints. Primary headaches (90%+) include migraine, tension-type headache (TTH), and trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (cluster headache). Tension-type headache: bilateral, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate intensity, not aggravated by activity, no nausea (may have photophobia OR phonophobia but not both); episodic (<15 days/month) or chronic (≥15 days/month). Cluster headache: severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain lasting 15-180 minutes, with ipsilateral autonomic features (ptosis, miosis, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, conjunctival injection), restlessness/agitation; occurs in clusters (weeks-months) with remission periods; more common in men, associated with smoking. Secondary headaches result from underlying pathology: subarachnoid hemorrhage (thunderclap), meningitis (fever, nuchal rigidity), giant cell arteritis (age >50, jaw claudication, elevated ESR), idiopathic intracranial hypertension (obese women of childbearing age, papilledema), CSF leak (orthostatic), brain tumor (progressive, worse in morning), medication overuse headache.

Headache treatment depends on type. Tension-type headache: acute treatment with simple analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs); avoid opioids. Chronic TTH prevention: amitriptyline (first-line, 10-75mg at bedtime), mirtazapine, venlafaxine; non-pharmacologic (stress management, physical therapy, acupuncture, biofeedback). Cluster headache acute treatment: high-flow oxygen (100% at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather for 15-20 minutes—first-line, safe, effective), subcutaneous sumatriptan 6mg (rapid onset, can repeat once in 24 hours), intranasal sumatriptan or zolmitriptan. Cluster prevention: verapamil (first-line, start 80mg TID, titrate up to 480-960mg/day, requires ECG monitoring for heart block), galcanezumab (monthly injection for episodic cluster), lithium (second-line, monitor levels/thyroid/renal), topiramate. Transitional prevention: prednisone taper (60mg × 5 days then taper) or greater occipital nerve block (steroid + local anesthetic) to bridge until preventive takes effect. Refractory cases: occipital nerve stimulation, sphenopalatine ganglion stimulation, deep brain stimulation (hypothalamus—last resort). Medication overuse headache: withdraw offending medication (abruptly for triptans/NSAIDs, taper for opioids/benzodiazepines), bridge with steroids/NSAIDs, initiate preventive.

Headache diagnosis uses ICHD-3 criteria and clinical evaluation. Tension-type headache: ≥10 episodes with ≥2 of: bilateral location, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate intensity, not aggravated by routine activity; no nausea/vomiting, ≤1 of photophobia/phonophobia. Cluster headache: ≥5 attacks with severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain lasting 15-180 minutes untreated, plus ≥1 ipsilateral autonomic symptom (conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, forehead/facial sweating, miosis, ptosis, eyelid edema) and/or sense of restlessness. Red flags requiring imaging/laboratory workup (SNOOP4): Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats), Neurologic deficit (focal signs, altered consciousness, papilledema), Onset sudden (thunderclap—peak <1 minute), Onset after age 50 (consider GCA—check ESR/CRP), Pattern change (progressive worsening), Positional headache (worse upright suggests CSF leak; worse supine suggests IIH), Precipitated by Valsalva, Pregnancy/postpartum (CVST, preeclampsia, RCVS), Painful eye with autonomic features (rule out acute angle-closure glaucoma), Post-traumatic onset. Diagnostic tests: ESR/CRP for GCA, LP for IIH (opening pressure >25 cm H2O) or meningitis, MRI/MRV for structural/vascular causes, CT for acute hemorrhage.
